meta.title: Feature service state vs NgRx Store for a shared Angular workbench
meta.summary: Several Angular screens now share filters, selections, optimistic actions, and restore-on-navigation behavior. Which state approach would you defend?

scenario: You are designing an Angular operations workbench with a list view, a details drawer, bulk actions, saved filters, optimistic row updates, and restore-on-navigation behavior. Several engineers work on the feature at the same time. In the interview, the panel asks whether you would keep the shared state in a feature service with RxJS or signals, or move the core shared state into NgRx Store.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other option is still the better answer?

options.label: Feature service state
options.summary: A strong answer when the feature is still fairly contained and the team can keep state boundaries understandable without introducing store-wide ceremony.
whenItWins: The feature is mostly one screen or one workflow, and the shared state surface is still modest.
A small team wants quick iteration with a service, a few observables or signals, and clear local ownership.
The product does not need much global coordination, time-travel-style debugging, or reducer and effect conventions.
watchOutFor: As screens, optimistic flows, and parallel engineers grow, service boundaries can drift and debugging gets harder to explain.
A weak answer says 'a service is simpler' without explaining how async rules and shared contracts stay predictable over time.

options.label: NgRx Store
options.summary: The easier answer to defend once the feature has broad shared state, real async coordination, and multiple engineers needing predictable patterns.
whenItWins: Several screens share the same source of truth, and actions, selectors, and effects clarify how state changes over time.
Optimistic updates, retries, background loads, or restore-on-navigation behavior make explicit event and reducer patterns valuable.
The team benefits from clearer conventions, memoized selectors, and a more structured debugging story.
watchOutFor: It can sound heavier than necessary if the feature is still small and mostly local to one screen.
A weak answer says 'NgRx because enterprise' without tying the choice to this exact prompt.

decisionMatrix.title: Contained feature with modest shared state
decisionMatrix.prompt: One main screen, a small team, and a manageable amount of async behavior.

cells.note: This is very defendable when the state still feels local to one feature boundary and the team can keep it disciplined.
Still fine, but you need to explain why the extra structure pays off this early.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: Several screens, optimistic updates, saved filters, restore-on-navigation, and several engineers touching the same flows.

cells.note: Possible, but you now need strong discipline around boundaries, selectors, and async orchestration.
This is the easiest answer to defend when the feature already behaves like a small product within the app.

decisionMatrix.title: Long-lived feature with more teams and workflows
decisionMatrix.prompt: More screens, more side effects, more debugging pressure, and more engineers over time.

cells.note: At this point the answer starts to sound optimistic unless the service architecture is unusually disciplined.
The reducer, selector, and effect model becomes easier to defend as the feature keeps growing.

evaluationDimensions.title: How broad is the shared state surface?
evaluationDimensions.description: The first question is whether state stays local to one feature area or now spreads across several screens and interactions.

evaluationDimensions.title: How complex is the async behavior?
evaluationDimensions.description: Optimistic writes, retries, background refreshes, and restore-on-navigation are where state architecture starts to matter more.

evaluationDimensions.title: How many engineers need the same conventions?
evaluationDimensions.description: The bigger the team, the more valuable explicit patterns and predictable data flow become.

evaluationDimensions.title: How easy is it to explain and trace state changes?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer considers how another engineer will follow the flow of actions, selectors, and state updates later.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is the extra structure worth it yet?
evaluationDimensions.description: A balanced answer also asks whether the feature is already large enough for store-level ceremony to pay off.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean NgRx Store, but only because this feature already looks like a shared product surface
strongAnswer.summary: This prompt describes several screens, real async coordination, and multiple engineers working in the same state flows. That makes NgRx the easier answer to defend for the core shared feature state.
reasoning: The feature no longer feels like one local screen. It has shared filters, selections, restore-on-navigation behavior, and optimistic updates across multiple views.
That shape makes actions, reducers, selectors, and effects easier to explain than an increasingly large service with ad hoc conventions.
A feature service is still a good answer for smaller or calmer features, so the choice is tied to the scale and coordination pressure in this prompt.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would lean NgRx for the core shared feature state, while still keeping very local view state such as panel-open flags or transient hover state out of the store.

interviewerPushback.question: Why not keep it simple with a feature service and a few observables or signals?
interviewerPushback.answer: I would be comfortable with that if the feature were more contained. In this prompt, the state now spans several screens and several engineers, so I care more about explicit conventions and easier debugging than the lightest possible setup.

interviewerPushback.question: Is NgRx too much ceremony for one feature?
interviewerPushback.answer: It can be, which is why I would not default to it for every Angular screen. Here the feature already has enough shared behavior and async coordination that the extra structure feels justified rather than premature.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If the feature shrank back toward one screen with calmer async behavior and only a small team touching it, I would move the answer toward a feature service to keep the architecture lighter.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would use NgRx because large Angular apps should use NgRx.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This sounds generic and skips the actual tradeoff in the prompt.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably use NgRx because the feature is getting bigger, but a feature service could also work if the team keeps it clean.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs more concrete reasoning about shared screens and async flows.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would lean NgRx for the core shared feature state because the workbench already has several screens, optimistic updates, restore-on-navigation behavior, and multiple engineers touching the same flows. A feature service is still a good choice for smaller and calmer features, but this one has enough coordination pressure that explicit store patterns become easier to defend.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds stronger because it starts from the shape of the feature, not from a tool preference, and it still gives a fair boundary for the lighter option.

answerFramework: Start by naming the current scale of the feature: one local screen or a shared product surface.
State which state approach you would choose for this prompt.
Explain two or three concrete constraints that drive the choice, such as async coordination, multiple screens, or team scale.
Acknowledge when the lighter option is still the right answer.
End with a clear boundary, for example: NgRx for core shared feature state, local state for transient UI details.
antiPatterns: Saying NgRx is always the Angular answer because serious apps use stores.
Saying a service is always simpler without explaining how conventions hold up as the feature grows.
Putting every tiny UI flag in the store without discussing scope.
Talking only about boilerplate and ignoring debuggability, selectors, and async coordination.
